patches_reviewed.subject: Re: Simple quota unsafe? RIP: 0010:__btrfs_free_extent.isra.0+0xc41/0x1020 [btrfs] / do_free_extent_accounting:2999: errno=-2 No such entry
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch is a response to an issue where the btrfs file system is experiencing errors when trying to free extents, resulting in a '-2 No such entry' error. The developer, Boris Burkov, is seeking more information about how the quota was being managed before the error occurred, specifically whether subvolumes or qgroups were manually deleted.

review_comments.author: Marc MERLIN
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the system was experiencing crashes due to btrfs quota issues, and provided details about their specific setup, including nightly snapshots, subvolume deletion, and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots. They mentioned that they have quotas still running on a single host but turned off balance and scrub jobs as a workaround.
review_comments.raw_body:
All 3 filesystems this happened on were btrfs receive recipients, so
that created snapshots nightly (when it crashed each time).
Also after btrfs receive, my script deletes the oldest backup that rolls
out the rotation.
Then I also have some amount btrfs-snaps that makes hourly/daily/weekly
snapshots

So yes, multiple per day per subvolume multipled by many subvolume
(10-ish)

I have squotas still running on a single host but I turned off btrfs
balance nightly cronjob as well as btrfs scrub, and it has not crashed
yet (it's a 20TB+ backup recipient built from scratch, I woudl love for
squotas to work on it)

Marc
-- 
"A mouse is a device used to point at the xterm you want to type in" - A.S.R.
 
Home page: http://marc.merlins.org/                       | PGP 7F55D5F27AAF9D08

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: Simple quota unsafe? RIP: 0010:__btrfs_free_extent.isra.0+0xc41/0x1020 [btrfs] / do_free_extent_accounting:2999: errno=-2 No such entry
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch is a response to an issue where the btrfs file system is experiencing errors when trying to free extents, resulting in a '-2 No such entry' error. The developer, Boris Burkov, is seeking more information about how the quota was being managed before the error occurred, specifically whether subvolumes or qgroups were manually deleted.

review_comments.author: Marc MERLIN
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Marc MERLIN encountered an error when trying to remove the quota on a Btrfs filesystem, receiving an errno=-2 No such entry error in do_free_extent_accounting. He was able to resolve the issue by canceling the balance operation and then re-converting the filesystem to block group trees.
review_comments.raw_body:
Took a while to remove the quota, but once it did:

moremagic:/tmp# mount -o rw,enospc_debug,skip_balance /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 /mnt/btrfs_bigbackup
moremagic:/tmp# cd /mnt/btrfs_bigbackup
moremagic:/mnt/btrfs_bigbackup# btrfs balance cancel `pwd`
BTRFS info (device dm-0): setting compat-ro feature flag for FREE_SPACE_TREE (0x1)
BTRFS info (device dm-0): setting compat-ro feature flag for FREE_SPACE_TREE_VALID (0x2)
BTRFS info (device dm-0): balance: resume skipped
BTRFS info (device dm-0): checking UUID tree
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling ssd optimizations
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling free space tree
BTRFS info (device dm-0): balance: canceled

And now I'm good. I can re-re-convert to block group trees (took hours
overnight to go back):
moremagic:~# btrfstune --convert-to-block-group-tree  /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0
Converted the filesystem to block group tree feature

moremagic:~# mount /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 /mnt/btrfs_bigbackup
BTRFS: device label DS6 devid 1 transid 298273 /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 (251:0) scanned by mount (1030419)
BTRFS info (device dm-0): first mount of filesystem a97dec85-a0d5-42ab-a0ef-e9b7479fbe43
BTRFS info (device dm-0): using crc32c (crc32c-generic) checksum algorithm
BTRFS info (device dm-0): forcing free space tree for sector size 4096 with page size 16384
BTRFS warning (device dm-0): read-write for sector size 4096 with page size 16384 is experimental
BTRFS info (device dm-0): bdev /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 errs: wr 0, rd 0, flush 0, corrupt 5074, gen 0
BTRFS info (device dm-0): checking UUID tree
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling ssd optimizations
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling free space tree

I'm back in business, thank you.

Will definitely keep squota off for this one. Thanks again about the
--remove-simple-quota suggestion, of all the things I tried, this is one
I hadn't.

Marc
-- 
"A mouse is a device used to point at the xterm you want to type in" - A.S.R.
 
Home page: http://marc.merlins.org/                       | PGP 7F55D5F27AAF9D08

review_comments.author: Marc MERLIN
review_comments.summary: The reviewer, Marc MERLIN, observed that running btrfs balance on a filesystem with quota enabled caused a -2 No such entry error, leading to a transaction abort and the filesystem being put into readonly mode. He noted that removing quota resolved the issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
No help needed, just showing it happened overnight with balance and squota in 6.19
on my laptop. It looks like squota and balance are simply not compatible until the
fix for squota, lands.
Now that I know more, I rescued the FS and removed squota on my own and it's good again.

It looks pretty clear that btrfs balance seems to trigger the bug in
squota:
[37930.185472] BTRFS info (device dm-3): balance: start -dusage=20
[37930.187010] BTRFS info (device dm-3): relocating block group 4502498312192 flags data
[37930.417388] BTRFS info (device dm-3): found 83 extents, stage: move data extents

sauron:/boot# mount /dev/mapper/pool4 /mnt/btrfs_pool4
[65964.162801] BTRFS error (device dm-3 state EMA): remounting read-write after error is not allowed

For google and people who find that thread, the quicker recovery is:
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# btrfs rescue clear-ino-cache  /dev/mapper/pool4
(...)
Successfully cleaned up ino cache for root id: 108518
Successfully cleared ino cache
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# mount -o rw,clear_cache,enospc_debug,skip_balance LABEL=btrfs_pool4 /mnt/btrfs_pool4
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# btrfs quota disable /mnt/btrfs_pool4

sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# mount /dev/mapper/pool4 /mnt/btrfs_pool4
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# cd /mnt/btrfs_pool4
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool4# btrfs balance start -dusage=20  `pwd`
Done, had to relocate 0 out of 2008 chunks
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool4# 

btrfs tune would have likely also worked to remove squota, but the trick
after that is mounting with skip_balance or otherwise, even after quota
removal, balance restarts right away and puts the FS read only again
if squota isn't disabled first.

Marc
-- 
"A mouse is a device used to point at the xterm you want to type in" - A.S.R.
 
Home page: http://marc.merlins.org/                       | PGP 7F55D5F27AAF9D08

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author is seeking clarification on whether reviewer was manually managing subvolumes or qgroups at the time of error, indicating a desire to reproduce the issue accurately.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thank you for the additional report, this is helpful. I am attempting to
reproduce directly with balance to be sure I've got everything.

One question just to be extra careful:
In all these situations, were you ever manually managing either the
subvolumes or the qgroups? e.g., when, if ever, where you deleting
qgroups and / or subvols?

Thanks,
Boris

The developer, Boris Burkov, is seeking more information about how the quota was being managed before the error occurred, specifically whether subvolumes or qgroups were manually deleted.

review_comments.author: Marc MERLIN
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Marc MERLIN reported that attempting to remove simple quota using btrfstune --remove-simple-quota resulted in an error, specifically bad eb member end errors, and the process was still running after a while.

Reviewer Marc MERLIN expressed concern that the patch may not be sufficient to fix the quota issue, and suggested running btrfs tune --remove-simple-quota to potentially resolve the problem before attempting further checks or modifications.
review_comments.raw_body:
moremagic:/tmp# btrfstune --remove-simple-quota /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0
bad eb member end: ptr 0x4000 start 15495725432832 member offset 16384 size 1
bad eb member end: ptr 0x4000 start 16568941559808 member offset 16384 size 1
bad eb member end: ptr 0x4000 start 16133011357696 member offset 16384 size 1
bad eb member end: ptr 0x4000 start 15495760642048 member offset 16384 size 1
bad eb member end: ptr 0x4000 start 15641229164544 member offset 16384 size 1
bad eb member end: ptr 0x4000 start 16027775762432 member offset 16384 size 1
bad eb member end: ptr 0x4000 start 16217851576320 member offset 16384 size 1
bad eb member end: ptr 0x4000 start 16217853050880 member offset 16384 size 1
bad eb member end: ptr 0x4000 start 15505960157184 member offset 16384 size 1
(...) many lines

It's still running after a while and giving hundreds of those lines.

Willl report back when it's done.

---

thank you. But I can only check that if btrfs tune doesn't fix it. Did
you want me to stop the tune or let it run to completion?

Marc
-- 
"A mouse is a device used to point at the xterm you want to type in" - A.S.R.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that the issue is not immediately clear and suggests letting the test continue, indicating no immediate plan to fix the problem.
review_comments.raw_body:
Not too promising, but no theory what would cause that off the top of my
head. You can let it run, now that you started it, I think.

review_comments.author: Marc MERLIN
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Marc MERLIN encountered an error when trying to remove the quota on a Btrfs filesystem, receiving an errno=-2 No such entry error in do_free_extent_accounting. He was able to resolve the issue by canceling the balance operation and then re-converting the filesystem to block group trees.
review_comments.raw_body:
Took a while to remove the quota, but once it did:

moremagic:/tmp# mount -o rw,enospc_debug,skip_balance /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 /mnt/btrfs_bigbackup
moremagic:/tmp# cd /mnt/btrfs_bigbackup
moremagic:/mnt/btrfs_bigbackup# btrfs balance cancel `pwd`
BTRFS info (device dm-0): setting compat-ro feature flag for FREE_SPACE_TREE (0x1)
BTRFS info (device dm-0): setting compat-ro feature flag for FREE_SPACE_TREE_VALID (0x2)
BTRFS info (device dm-0): balance: resume skipped
BTRFS info (device dm-0): checking UUID tree
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling ssd optimizations
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling free space tree
BTRFS info (device dm-0): balance: canceled

And now I'm good. I can re-re-convert to block group trees (took hours
overnight to go back):
moremagic:~# btrfstune --convert-to-block-group-tree  /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0
Converted the filesystem to block group tree feature

moremagic:~# mount /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 /mnt/btrfs_bigbackup
BTRFS: device label DS6 devid 1 transid 298273 /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 (251:0) scanned by mount (1030419)
BTRFS info (device dm-0): first mount of filesystem a97dec85-a0d5-42ab-a0ef-e9b7479fbe43
BTRFS info (device dm-0): using crc32c (crc32c-generic) checksum algorithm
BTRFS info (device dm-0): forcing free space tree for sector size 4096 with page size 16384
BTRFS warning (device dm-0): read-write for sector size 4096 with page size 16384 is experimental
BTRFS info (device dm-0): bdev /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 errs: wr 0, rd 0, flush 0, corrupt 5074, gen 0
BTRFS info (device dm-0): checking UUID tree
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling ssd optimizations
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling free space tree

I'm back in business, thank you.

Will definitely keep squota off for this one. Thanks again about the
--remove-simple-quota suggestion, of all the things I tried, this is one
I hadn't.

Marc

review_comments.author: Marc MERLIN
review_comments.summary: The reviewer, Marc MERLIN, observed that running btrfs balance on a filesystem with quota enabled caused a -2 No such entry error, leading to a transaction abort and the filesystem being put into readonly mode. He noted that removing quota resolved the issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
No help needed, just showing it happened overnight with balance and squota in 6.19
on my laptop. It looks like squota and balance are simply not compatible until the
fix for squota, lands.
Now that I know more, I rescued the FS and removed squota on my own and it's good again.

It looks pretty clear that btrfs balance seems to trigger the bug in
squota:

sauron:/boot# mount /dev/mapper/pool4 /mnt/btrfs_pool4
[65964.162801] BTRFS error (device dm-3 state EMA): remounting read-write after error is not allowed

For google and people who find that thread, the quicker recovery is:
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# btrfs rescue clear-ino-cache  /dev/mapper/pool4
(...)
Successfully cleaned up ino cache for root id: 108518
Successfully cleared ino cache
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# mount -o rw,clear_cache,enospc_debug,skip_balance LABEL=btrfs_pool4 /mnt/btrfs_pool4
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# btrfs quota disable /mnt/btrfs_pool4

sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# mount /dev/mapper/pool4 /mnt/btrfs_pool4
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# cd /mnt/btrfs_pool4
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool4# btrfs balance start -dusage=20  `pwd`
Done, had to relocate 0 out of 2008 chunks
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool4# 

btrfs tune would have likely also worked to remove squota, but the trick
after that is mounting with skip_balance or otherwise, even after quota
removal, balance restarts right away and puts the FS read only again
if squota isn't disabled first.

Marc
-- 
"A mouse is a device used to point at the xterm you want to type in" - A.S.R.
 
Home page: http://marc.merlins.org/                       | PGP 7F55D5F27AAF9D08

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author is seeking clarification on whether reviewer was manually managing subvolumes or qgroups at the time of error, indicating a desire to reproduce the issue accurately.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thank you for the additional report, this is helpful. I am attempting to
reproduce directly with balance to be sure I've got everything.

One question just to be extra careful:
In all these situations, were you ever manually managing either the
subvolumes or the qgroups? e.g., when, if ever, where you deleting
qgroups and / or subvols?

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Marc MERLIN
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the system was experiencing crashes due to btrfs quota issues, and provided details about their specific setup, including nightly snapshots, subvolume deletion, and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots. They mentioned that they have quotas still running on a single host but turned off balance and scrub jobs as a workaround.
review_comments.raw_body:
All 3 filesystems this happened on were btrfs receive recipients, so
that created snapshots nightly (when it crashed each time).
Also after btrfs receive, my script deletes the oldest backup that rolls
out the rotation.
Then I also have some amount btrfs-snaps that makes hourly/daily/weekly
snapshots

So yes, multiple per day per subvolume multipled by many subvolume
(10-ish)

I have squotas still running on a single host but I turned off btrfs
balance nightly cronjob as well as btrfs scrub, and it has not crashed
yet (it's a 20TB+ backup recipient built from scratch, I woudl love for
squotas to work on it)

Marc
-- 
"A mouse is a device used to point at the xterm you want to type in" - A.S.R.
 
Home page: http://marc.merlins.org/                       | PGP 7F55D5F27AAF9D08

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 7/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qrwlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 7/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qrwlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to queued rwlocks, allowing for tracing of lock releases that occur while the lock is being contended by other threads. The implementation uses an out-of-line traced unlock approach similar to that used for queued spinlocks. This allows for more detailed debugging and analysis of concurrent access patterns in the kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 6/7] locking: Factor out __queued_read_unlock()/__queued_write_unlock()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch refactors the __queued_read_unlock() and __queued_write_unlock() functions out of the queued rwlock implementation, preparing for the addition of contended release tracepoint instrumentation.

review_comments.author: Paul E. McKenney
review_comments.summary: Acked the patch, indicating no objections or concerns.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to the qspinlock implementation, allowing for tracing of lock contention events. The tracepoint is implemented using the arch-overridable queued_spin_release() function, which ensures correct behavior across all architectures. The patch also includes changes to the hot path to minimize performance impact and provides binary size estimates.

review_comments.author: Paul E. McKenney
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch, acknowledging that it's a nice split out of functionality.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 4/7] locking: Factor out queued_spin_release()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function called `queued_spin_release()` which is an arch-overridable unlock primitive. It renames existing arch-specific overrides of `queued_spin_unlock()` to use the new name, making it easier for future changes to be made without affecting existing code. The change does not introduce any functional differences and is intended as a preparatory step for further modifications.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 2/7] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch extracts the slowpath of percpu_up_read() into a new function __percpu_up_read(). The original function is simplified and no longer contains the slowpath, which is moved to the new function. This change avoids increasing binary size due to the addition of a tracepoint in an inline function. The new function is exported as a symbol for use elsewhere in the kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The contended_release tracepoint fires on the holder side when a contended lock is released, allowing for correlation of lock hold time under contention with waiter events by lock address. The patch adds calls to the trace_contended_release() function in the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks, including mutexes, rtmutexes, semaphores, rwsems, and percpu-rwsems. This enables more detailed tracing and debugging of locking behavior in the kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 1/7] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 1/7] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes an unnecessary include statement from the lock tracing header file, as none of the trace events in this file reference anything from linux/sched.h.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/7] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides additional information such as hold time estimation and the holder's stack at release time, which can be used to measure how long the lock was held under contention. The patch also updates the behavior of reader/writer locks to fire the tracepoint for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, not just the last reader. The series includes several preparatory commits to split and refactor existing code.

review_comments.author: Paul McKenney
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] lockdep: fix NULL pointer dereference in __lock_set_class()
Re: [PATCH] locking: fix typos in comments

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
Re: [PATCH V10 00/10] famfs: port into fuse
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch removes the requirement for FUSE_DAX_FMAP to be advertised in perpetuity, even if CONFIG_FUSE_DAX is disabled. It uses capability bits to control support for DAX, similar to cpuid capability bits. The goal is to prevent over-genericization of the interface and allow for more targeted solutions.

review_comments.author: Dan Williams
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Dan Williams suggested that instead of making the interface more generic, the kernel should clearly indicate which methods are available and allow for a deprecation period before removing native support. He proposed adding a capability bit or similar mechanism to make it clear that native support might be removed in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
Right, if the protocol on day one is "user space must ask which method is available", then userspace can not be surprised when one option disappears. So to give time for the bpf approach to mature the kernel can do something like "famfs and bpf  mapping support are available". In some future kernel the famfs native option disappears after a deprecation period. 

When folks ask 10 years from now why this ever supported optionality the explanation is "oh because famfs enjoyed first mover advantage to prove out fs semantics layered on dax devices", or "turns out there are some cases where bpf is not fast enough but it still stops the proliferation of more in kernel mapping implementations".

Something like FUSE_DAX_FMAP is always available but the backend to that is optionally native vs bpf. ...or some other arrangement to make it clear that native might be gone someday.

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong expressed concern that the patch could break fuse servers if it removes GET_FMAP in favor of IOMAP_BEGIN without also updating libfuse to translate ->get_fmap implementations into ->iomap_begin, which would require dealing with vendored libfuse versions.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that famfs requires the ability to express formulaic meta-mapping, which cannot be efficiently achieved using traditional iomap parlance and individual iomaps; he believes there's no good reason to remove GET_FMAP at this time.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested a different approach to resolving the interface generality issue by merging fuse-iomap after famfs and extending the existing fuse-iomap-bpf prototype to allow per-mount and per-inode iomap bpf ops. This would enable thorough analysis of performance characteristics for various approaches, including using GET_FMAP as-is, uploading raw iomaps, or uploading custom bpf programs.

reviewer emphasized the importance of testing and gathering user feedback before making changes to the interface, suggesting that shipping the new mechanism to users is necessary for determining the best approach
review_comments.raw_body:
So don't break fuse servers.  If you wanted to (say) get rid of
GET_FMAP in favor of IOMAP_BEGIN, you could alter libfuse to translate a
fuse server's ->get_fmap implementation into the equivalent
->iomap_begin, and eventually the kernel can stop making GET_FMAP calls
to userspace.

The trouble here is that I've also seen half a dozen projects vendoring
libfuse so that's a nightmare that will have to be dealt with.  But
maybe that doesn't even matter, because...

---

...the memory interleaving is a rather interesting quality of famfs.
There's no good way to express a formulaic meta-mapping in traditional
iomap parlance, and famfs needs that to interleave across memory
controllers/dimm boxen/whatever.  Throwing individual iomaps at the
kernel is a very inefficient way to do that.  So I don't think there's a
good reason to get rid of GET_FMAP at this time...

---

...however the strongest case (IMO) would be if (having merged famfs) we
then merge fuse-iomap after famfs.  Then we extend the existing
fuse-iomap-bpf prototype to allow per-mount and per-inode iomap bpf ops.
That enables us to analyze thoroughly the performance characteristics of:

a) Using GET_FMAP as-is

b) Uploading raw iomaps (HA)

c) Uploading a single bpf program to make iomaps, exchanging fmap-style
mapping data into a bpf map, and having the single bpf program walk
through the map

d) Uploading a custom bpf program per famfs file to make iomaps.  No
bpfmap required, but the setup and compilation are now much more complex

Then we'll finally know which approach is the best, having broken the
Gordian Knot of how to merge famfs and fuse-iomap.

If we decide that (c) or (d) are actually better, then guess what?  To
get any of the iomap functionality, you have to set an inode flag, and
that (FUSE_CAP_FAMFS && FUSE_CAP_IOMAP && FUSE_ATTR_IOMAP) is the signal
for "don't call GET_FMAP".  FUSE_CAP_FAMFS && (!FUSE_CAP_IOMAP ||
!FUSE_ATTR_IOMAP) means "call GET_FMAP".

Yes, we burn a couple of fuse command values to find out, but that's all.

(TBH I still dislike GET_DAXDEV, that really should just be another
application of backing files, and the backing file id gets passed to
GET_FMAP.)

What do you all think of doing that?

---

Yes.  We're not *capable* of determining the best mechanism unless we
can start shipping these things to users to get their feedback.  Only
then can we iterate and make real improvements.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Joanne Koong expressed concern that relying on capability bits for deprecation may not be sufficient, as it does not address the issue of breaking users who are on older versions of libfuse if they upgrade to a newer kernel. She also questioned the deprecation period and process, asking what timeframe is required before a feature can be fully removed.

Reviewer Joanne Koong suggested making the interleaving part of the interface generic by adding an interleave descriptor to the user API, citing examples from other storage technologies such as RAID-0 and md-stripe.

Reviewer Joanne Koong expressed concerns that the GET_FMAP interface is too specific and may not be suitable for other interleaved or striped layouts, requesting it to be made more generic.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think it's this simple. We can't assume libfuse is the only
way servers talk to the kernel. Some servers use the /dev/fuse
interface directly. And, as I understand it, this would still break
users who are on older versions of libfuse if they upgrade to a newer
kernel.

My reason for pushing back isn't because I don't want this to work; I
just want to make sure that if we're going to rely on this as a safety
hatch, then we can actually do it.

Going back to what Dan said about using the capability bits for
deprecation, "In some future kernel the famfs native option disappears
after a deprecation period" - what does the deprecation period/process
look like? Do you have to wait a certain amount of time before it can
be fully removed or is it pretty immediate?

---

So could we make the interleaving part generic then? Striped /
interleaved layouts are used elsewhere (eg RAID-0, md-stripe, etc.) -
could we add a generic interleave descriptor to the uapi and use that
for what famfs needs?

---

To be completely honest, this is orthogonal to what I was hoping we
could discuss on this thread. My main concern is the GET_FMAP part.
Can we make it more generic to other interleaved/striped layouts?

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Joanne Koong questioned whether the interface is already generic and reusable for other dax-backed servers, despite being named famfs-specific, citing concerns that the naming conventions are implementation-specific.

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned the feasibility of making the interface more generic, citing concerns about performance overhead and the difficulty of finding a one-size-fits-all solution.

Reviewer Darrick Wong questioned whether a name change and capability bit could solve the issue of over-genericizing the interface, pointing out that md-raid presents a unified LBA address space, allowing filesystems to be unaware of translations underneath it.

Reviewer questioned the need for an overly generic interface, citing lack of evidence that it's actually needed and expressing concern that it would invite over-generalization.
review_comments.raw_body:
That depends on how much gluecode you can stand up to redirect older
programs.

---

I doubt it.  md-raid presents a unified LBA address space, which means
that the filesystem doesn't have to know anything about whatever
translations might happen underneath it.  Even memory controllers
quietly take care of striping across DIMMs and whatnot.

Most filesystems that implement striping themselves don't restrict
themselves to monotonically increasing LBA ranges rotored across each
device like md-raid0 does.

But for whatever reason, pmem/dax don't have remapping layers like
md/dm so filesystems have to do that on their own if the hardware
doesn't do it for them.

---

"Generic"... do we even /have/ a second user?  I don't feel like we do.

--D

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
